Restaurant Window Cleaning in BC: A Complete Guide for Food Service Operators

Published 2026-05-31 · Wagon Windows

Restaurant windows get dirtier faster than any other commercial glass — and they matter more to customers than almost any other business type. From kitchen exhaust grease to daily fingerprint accumulation on entry doors, food service operations face a window cleaning challenge that's specific enough to warrant its own framework. Here's what BC restaurant operators need to know.

Need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Get an Instant Estimate (403) 837-5092

Why Restaurant Windows Are a Unique Cleaning Challenge

Restaurant glass faces contamination sources that don't exist in any other commercial category. Understanding these specific challenges is the starting point for managing them effectively. **Kitchen exhaust grease**: Commercial kitchen ventilation exhausts air through roof-mounted or wall-mounted hoods that often vent near windows or allow ambient grease-laden air to circulate outdoors. This grease deposits on exterior glass as a fine, invisible film that's chemically different from dust or mineral deposits. Grease film on glass creates a hazy, smeared appearance when it catches light, and it actively attracts and holds other particulate — pollen, dust, combustion byproducts from nearby vehicles. Standard window cleaning solutions don't cut grease well; a degreasing pre-treatment is needed for kitchen-adjacent windows. **Entry glass fingerprints**: A busy restaurant's entry door glass is touched by every person who enters or exits. A lunch service with 50 covers generates at least 100 hand-contact events on the entry glass. Multiply that by dinner service and a full week, and entry glass accumulates an extraordinary fingerprint load. This is the most visible window in the building from a customer's perspective — it's the last thing they see before entering and the first thing they see on exit. **Condensation and cooking steam**: Restaurants with visible kitchen areas, pass-through windows to kitchen stations, or steam-intensive cooking produce ambient moisture that deposits on interior glass surfaces. This combines with airborne grease to create a film on interior surfaces that's distinctly different from the exterior contamination challenge.

  • Grease film from kitchen exhaust: requires degreasing pre-treatment, not just standard window cleaning
  • Entry door fingerprints: 100+ contact events per day in a moderately busy restaurant
  • Condensation and steam: deposits on interior glass near kitchen areas
  • Patio window exposure: heavy use, finger contact, and outdoor particulate during service periods

How Often Restaurant Windows Should Be Cleaned

The right cleaning frequency for a restaurant depends on two variables: the contamination rate (how fast the glass gets dirty) and the presentation stakes (how much the glass condition matters to your specific customer experience). For entry door glass, weekly cleaning is the realistic minimum for any restaurant with meaningful lunch and dinner service. Entry glass is the most-touched, most-noticed surface in the building from a customer perspective. A fingerprint-covered entry door communicates low hygiene standards before the customer has touched a menu. In peak season for Shuswap tourism restaurants — June through August — bi-weekly entry glass cleaning is often justified. For dining room window glass and storefront glass facing the street, monthly professional cleaning is appropriate for most Salmon Arm restaurants. During summer tourist season, bi-weekly cleans maintain the standard better. The street-facing glass gets grease film and pollen buildup faster in summer due to vehicle traffic and seasonal particulate, and it's viewed by potential new customers who are making entry decisions. Kitchen-adjacent exterior glass — windows near the exhaust hood or kitchen ventilation — may need more frequent cleaning depending on the grease output of your kitchen. If you're running a high-volume kitchen with a fryer, roaster, or grill, the windows within five meters of exhaust points will accumulate grease film measurably faster than other glass. Patio windows and glass-panel railings used during summer service are a separate consideration. These get heavy outdoor use and fingerprint contact during patio service hours and may need mid-week touch-ups in addition to a regular professional visit schedule.

  • Entry door glass: weekly minimum, bi-weekly in peak season
  • Storefront and dining room windows: monthly standard, bi-weekly during tourist season
  • Kitchen-adjacent exterior glass: frequency depends on kitchen grease output
  • Patio glass and railings: assess separately based on summer service hours

Health Inspection Optics and Professional Standards

In British Columbia, food service establishments are subject to inspection by Fraser Health, Interior Health, or the relevant regional health authority. While window cleanliness is not a scored inspection category on its own, the overall impression of hygiene and maintenance that inspectors form — and that they note in qualitative assessments — is influenced by the full visual environment, including window condition. More directly, health inspections create 'inspection pressure' events that make restaurant operators suddenly aware of presentation standards throughout the building. A restaurant that is generally well-maintained doesn't need to scramble before an inspection. A restaurant that defers maintenance — including window cleaning — finds itself doing emergency cleaning before inspection dates. The practical recommendation for any BC restaurant operator is to maintain windows as part of your standard hygiene regime rather than treating them as a cosmetic item to address irregularly. Monthly professional cleaning, complemented by daily staff wipe-downs of entry glass, keeps the building in inspection-ready condition as a baseline. For Shuswap restaurants that are reviewed online — Google, Yelp, TripAdvisor — window condition contributes to the review-worthy visual presentation of the establishment. Reviewers photograph restaurant exteriors and interiors. Dirty windows end up in photos. Those photos are associated with your Google Business profile. The reputational half-life of a bad photo outlasts the cleaning appointment it represents by months or years.

Online review photography is permanent. A dirty exterior in a reviewer's photo stays associated with your business listing long after the window has been cleaned. Maintain the standard, don't just catch up before inspections.

What a Proper Restaurant Window Service Includes

A professional restaurant window cleaning service should account for the specific contamination profile of a food service operation, not just apply the same approach used on an office or retail space. The scope of a proper restaurant service includes: **Exterior street-facing glass**: Full exterior clean including storefront windows, any display windows, and glazed panels adjacent to the entry. Grease film pre-treatment applied to kitchen-adjacent windows before the standard clean. **Entry door glass (both faces)**: Interior and exterior of all entry and exit doors. Fingerprint removal is the primary task here. This is often the highest-impact single element of a restaurant window clean and should never be skipped. **Interior dining room glass**: Windows and glazed partitions visible to diners. Condensation films and interior cooking deposit need to be addressed, not just the exterior surfaces. **Frames and sills**: Window frames accumulate grime that migrates to glass during the next rain event. A clean that doesn't address frames leaves the source of re-contamination in place. **Patio glass (seasonal)**: For restaurants with outdoor seating areas bounded by glass panels, seasonal patio glass cleaning as part of a service visit maintains the patio environment. Wagon Windows serves restaurants in Salmon Arm and throughout the Shuswap. We understand the specific contamination challenges of food service environments and schedule around your service hours — early morning before opening or in the early afternoon between lunch and dinner service — to minimize operational disruption.

  • Exterior glass: grease pre-treatment for kitchen-adjacent windows
  • Entry door glass: both faces, full fingerprint removal
  • Interior dining room glass: condensation and cooking deposit removal
  • Frames and sills: clean the source, not just the surface
  • Patio glass: seasonal cleaning integrated into regular service schedule

Frequently Asked Questions

How do you remove grease film from restaurant exterior windows?

Grease film requires a degreasing pre-treatment before standard window cleaning solution is applied. We use a diluted degreaser on affected panels, allow dwell time, then scrub with a soft sleeve before squeegee finishing. Standard window cleaning solution alone doesn't cut grease effectively and leaves a smeared result if the pre-treatment step is skipped.

Can you clean restaurant windows without disturbing service?

Yes. We schedule restaurant visits before opening (typically 7-9am) or between lunch and dinner service (2-4pm) to avoid customer contact. Exterior-only visits can often happen during service without disruption. For interior cleaning, we coordinate with the manager to work in sections that avoid active dining areas.

Will dirty restaurant windows affect my health inspection?

Window cleanliness is not a scored inspection item on BC health authority inspection forms. However, the overall hygiene impression matters to inspectors and is reflected in qualitative notes. More importantly, exterior restaurant photos taken by customers and reviewers end up associated with your online business listings — and dirty window photos have a long online lifespan.

Do you offer recurring contracts for restaurants?

Yes, and most of our restaurant clients are on recurring contracts — weekly for entry glass, monthly for full exterior, or a combined schedule that makes sense for their operation. Contract pricing is better than one-off pricing, and automatic scheduling means you never have to remember to book. Call (403) 837-5092 to discuss what schedule fits your restaurant.
